name: Submariner Date
description: The current-generation of the world's most iconic dive watch. Introduced in 2020 with calibre 3235 and 70-hour power reserve in a 41mm Oystersteel case with black Cerachrom bezel.
history: The Submariner is Rolex's defining sports watch, introduced in 1953 as the world's first watch water-resistant to 100m. The ref. 126610LN is the current generation, unveiled at Watches & Wonders 2020, featuring the in-house Calibre 3235 with a 70-hour power reserve — a significant leap over the previous 48-hour movement. Its Oystersteel case with a black Cerachrom ceramic bezel insert is virtually scratch-proof and UV-resistant. The 41mm case size marked an increase from the 40mm that defined the model for decades.
highestSalePrice: A vintage Rolex Submariner ref. 6538 'Big Crown' worn by Sean Connery on set during the James Bond films sold at auction for $365,000 at Christie's. Modern steel Submariners trade at significant premiums over their ~$10,500 retail price on the secondary market.
funFacts: The Submariner first appeared on James Bond's wrist in 'Dr. No' (1962) — making it the most famous spy watch in cinema history.
Early 'Submariner' was misspelled 'Sumbmariner' on some dials — those errors now sell for enormous premiums to collectors.
The Submariner was the first watch certified water-resistant to 100 meters (330 feet) when launched in 1953.
Rolex tests every Submariner under pressure equivalent to 300m depth before it leaves the factory.

name: GMT-Master II Pepsi
description: The iconic blue-red Cerachrom bezel GMT, originally designed for Pan Am pilots. Features calibre 3285 with redesigned GMT mechanism on Jubilee bracelet.
history: The GMT-Master was developed in collaboration with Pan American World Airways in 1954, allowing pilots to track two time zones simultaneously. The 'Pepsi' bezel — red for day hours, blue for night — became an instant icon of the jet age. The ref. 126710BLRO introduced in 2018 was the first GMT-Master II to feature the ceramic 'Pepsi' bezel, which Rolex had struggled to manufacture due to the challenge of creating a two-color ceramic insert. It pairs the Jubilee bracelet for the first time with this case.
highestSalePrice: A rare Rolex GMT-Master ref. 1675 'Pussy Galore' with a specific tropical dial sold at Phillips for $160,000. The ceramic Pepsi (126710BLRO) commands a consistent market premium, typically trading at $20,000–$25,000 on secondary markets versus ~$10,650 retail.
funFacts: The GMT-Master was originally co-developed with Pan American World Airways for their transoceanic crews — the airline bought the first batch.
The 'Pepsi' nickname for the red-blue bezel came from watch enthusiasts drawing a comparison to the soft drink's branding.
For many years, Rolex could not produce a two-tone ceramic bezel — the 'Pepsi' only came in aluminum until 2018 when Rolex finally solved the manufacturing challenge.
A separate GMT hand — the 24-hour hand — allows reading a second time zone independently of the main hour hand.

name: Cosmograph Daytona
description: The ultimate racing chronograph. Features calibre 4131, Cerachrom bezel with tachymetre scale, and the classic Panda dial. One of the most coveted watches in the world.
history: The Cosmograph Daytona traces its origins to the 1963 ref. 6239, named after the Daytona International Speedway in Florida. Designed for racing drivers who needed to measure elapsed time and average speed, it featured a tachymeter scale on the bezel. The watch languished in obscurity until Paul Newman was photographed wearing one — the so-called 'Paul Newman Daytona' became the most coveted vintage watch in the world. The ref. 126500LN (2021) is the first stainless steel Daytona with a black ceramic bezel, replacing the aluminum insert used since 2000.
highestSalePrice: Paul Newman's own Rolex Daytona ref. 6239, engraved 'Drive carefully, me' by his wife Joanne Woodward, sold at Phillips New York in October 2017 for $17,752,500 — a world record for any wristwatch at auction at the time. It remains one of the most storied auction sales in horology.
funFacts: When the Daytona launched in 1963, it was so unpopular that jewelers struggled to sell them — ironically making early examples extremely rare today.
Paul Newman received his Daytona ref. 6239 as a gift from his wife Joanne Woodward; she had engraved the caseback 'Drive carefully, me.'

The tachymeter bezel can be used to calculate average speeds: start the chronograph at a one-mile marker, and the seconds hand points to your average speed in miles per hour when the next mile marker is crossed.
The current Daytona has a six-year waiting list at authorized dealers in many markets, despite retailing for around $14,550.

name: Datejust 41
description: The quintessential Rolex, first introduced in 1945 as the first self-winding wristwatch with a date display. The current 41mm version features calibre 3235 with fluted bezel and Jubilee bracelet.
history: The Datejust is the most enduring design in Rolex's catalogue, introduced in 1945 as the first wristwatch to feature an automatically changing date display — the 'Cyclops' lens magnifying the date by 2.5x was added in 1953. Worn by world leaders, royalty, and millions of ordinary people, the Datejust is the quintessential dress-sports watch. The ref. 126334 in 41mm Rolesor (two-tone steel and white gold) continues the Datejust tradition with the Calibre 3235.
highestSalePrice: Datejust watches generally don't reach extraordinary auction heights as they are produced in high volumes, but vintage Datejust models with rare dials or configurations can sell for tens of thousands. A unique ref. 1601 Datejust with a lapis lazuli dial sold for $28,750 at Sotheby's.
funFacts: The Datejust was the first self-winding watch with an instantaneously jumping date display — a technical achievement in 1945 that changed wristwatches forever.
Queen Elizabeth II received a Datejust as a coronation gift from Rolex in 1953, reportedly wearing it to many state occasions.
The word 'Cyclops' for the date magnifying lens is a trademarked term by Rolex — the lens magnifies the date 2.5 times.
More Datejusts have been produced than any other Rolex model — estimates suggest over 5 million examples since 1945.

name: Explorer
description: Born from Rolex's association with Himalayan expeditions, the Explorer is the definitive tool watch. The current 36mm reference returns to the original size with calibre 3230.
history: The Explorer was born from Rolex's sponsorship of the 1953 British expedition to Mount Everest. While the exact model worn by Sir Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay on the summit has been debated by historians, Rolex's association with that achievement defined the Explorer's identity. The name 'Explorer' was trademarked by Rolex and the model positioned as a tool watch for adventurers. The ref. 124270 (2021) returned the Explorer to the classic 36mm size after a controversial move to 39mm in 2010.
highestSalePrice: Vintage Explorers with tropical dials and service records from famous expeditions command strong premiums. A rare ref. 1016 Explorer with a 'rail dial' sold for $52,000 at Phillips. The modern ref. 124270 retails at ~$6,800 but trades at $8,000–$10,000 on secondary markets.
funFacts: The Explorer's dial features only 3, 6, and 9 with no other hour markers — designed for ultimate legibility in extreme conditions.
Rolex supplied watches to the 1953 Everest expedition and the connection was so powerful that it shaped the brand's adventure image for decades.
The Explorer is considered by many collectors to be the purest expression of the Rolex design philosophy — no complications, no frills, just perfect timekeeping.
When Rolex controversially enlarged the Explorer to 39mm in 2010, the outcry from collectors was so strong that Rolex reverted to 36mm with the ref. 124270 in 2021.

name: Explorer II
description: Designed for speleologists and polar explorers with a fixed 24-hour bezel for distinguishing day from night. Features the calibre 3285 GMT movement in a 42mm Oystersteel case.
history: The Explorer II was introduced in 1971 designed for spelunkers (cave explorers) who needed to distinguish AM from PM underground, where there is no natural light cycle. Its distinctive 24-hour hand and fixed bezel serve that purpose. Later it became popular with scientists working at polar stations where extended daylight or darkness made conventional reading of time difficult. The ref. 226570 (2021) updated the classic design with the Calibre 3285 movement.
highestSalePrice: The Explorer II is more collectible in its vintage form; a rare ref. 1655 'Steve McQueen' Explorer II (so nicknamed because McQueen wore one in photos, though he actually preferred the Explorer I) sold for $175,000 at Antiquorum. Modern examples trade at modest premiums.
funFacts: The Explorer II was originally designed for cavers — spelunkers working underground who had no way to distinguish AM from PM without a 24-hour hand.
The model is nicknamed 'Freccione' (big arrow) in Italy due to its prominent orange 24-hour hand.
NASA scientists stationed at the South Pole used Explorer II watches because in Antarctic summers, the sun never sets — making a 24-hour hand essential.
Unlike the GMT-Master, the Explorer II's bezel is fixed — only the hand rotates — making it simpler and more rugged.

name: Day-Date 40
description: The 'Presidents' Watch', worn by world leaders since 1956. The first watch to display both day and date. This 18k Everose gold reference features the calibre 3255.
history: The Day-Date, launched in 1956, was the first watch to display the day of the week spelled out in full as well as the date. Available only in gold or platinum, it was positioned as Rolex's ultimate prestige timepiece and became known as the 'President's watch' after Dwight D. Eisenhower received one. The President bracelet — developed specifically for the Day-Date — became one of Rolex's most recognizable design elements. The ref. 228235 features the elegant Everose gold case.
highestSalePrice: A rare Rolex Day-Date 'Stella' with a vibrant enamel dial in turquoise sold at Phillips for $127,500. Day-Date watches in platinum with exotic stone dials regularly achieve $50,000–$150,000 at major auction houses.
funFacts: The Day-Date has never been made in steel — only gold or platinum — making it Rolex's most exclusive standard production model.
Lyndon B. Johnson was so fond of his gold Day-Date that he reportedly gave away dozens to friends, colleagues, and dignitaries during his presidency.
The 'President' bracelet name came directly from the Eisenhower association — Rolex marketing began calling it that after the President wore his Day-Date publicly.
Day-Date dials have been made from over 30 different materials including meteorite, malachite, onyx, lapis lazuli, and mother-of-pearl.

name: Yacht-Master 42
description: Rolex's sailing-inspired luxury sports watch in RLX titanium. Features a bidirectional rotatable bezel with raised numerals and calibre 3235.
history: The Yacht-Master line was introduced in 1992 as Rolex's sailing watch, sitting above the Submariner in prestige. The distinctive bidirectional rotating bezel with raised markings was designed for sailors to time race starts. The ref. 226627 in 42mm white gold with a gray 'Oysterflex' bracelet — Rolex's rubber strap with a metal spine — represents the pinnacle of the Yacht-Master range in sporty precious metal.
highestSalePrice: The Yacht-Master in precious metals retails at $35,000–$45,000 and holds value well. No single auction record stands out, but gold Yacht-Masters regularly trade well above retail on secondary markets.
funFacts: The Yacht-Master's bezel can rotate in both directions — unlike the Submariner's unidirectional bezel — because sailing requires countdown timing (bidirectional) more than a single direction lock.
The Oysterflex bracelet, made of metal and rubber, was developed by Rolex as an alternative to both metal bracelets and traditional rubber straps — it has a titanium-nickel alloy blade inside.
The Yacht-Master II (a separate model) features a programmable countdown regatta timer — the only Rolex with a fully programmable complication.
Rolex has been the official timekeeper of the America's Cup since 1995, cementing the Yacht-Master's nautical credentials.

name: Air-King
description: A tribute to aviation pioneers with its distinctive dial featuring oversized 3, 6, 9 numerals and minutes scale. Features the calibre 3230 in a 40mm Oystersteel case with crown guard.

specs.dialColor: Black with green and yellow accents

history: The Air-King was created in the 1940s to honor RAF (Royal Air Force) pilots who wore Rolex watches during World War II. It was the last watch to carry a historical Rolex name from that era in continuous production. The current ref. 126900 (2022) features a distinctive black dial with a large 'ROLEX' text and green seconds hand, along with a 40mm Oyster case. The bezel carries the minute track prominently as a nod to its aviation heritage.
highestSalePrice: Vintage Air-King models with rare configurations sell at $5,000–$25,000. The modern ref. 126900 retails at approximately $7,300 and generally trades near retail on the secondary market.
funFacts: The Air-King was designed specifically to honor British RAF fighter pilots — Rolex supplied subsidized watches to RAF aviators during WWII as a goodwill gesture.
The current Air-King shares its case and movement with the Milgauss — both are designed with anti-magnetic properties.
The prominent 'ROLEX' text on the dial in a large font is a unique feature not found on other Rolex sport models, making it instantly recognizable.
Rolex has retained the Air-King name in continuous production for over 75 years — longer than almost any other sports watch name in the industry.

name: Sky-Dweller
description: Rolex's most complex current production watch with annual calendar and dual time zone. The innovative Ring Command bezel system allows easy setting of all functions.
history: The Sky-Dweller, introduced in 2012, is Rolex's most complex watch in current production. It features an annual calendar (requiring only one manual correction per year), a dual time zone display with an off-center 24-hour disc, and a proprietary setting system called 'Ring Command' that uses the bezel to select which function to adjust. The movement, Calibre 9001, took Rolex a decade to develop and contains 14 patents.
highestSalePrice: The Sky-Dweller in precious metals can reach $30,000–$50,000 at retail; the steel version (ref. 336934 in Rolesor) retails at approximately $16,250 and trades at a premium of $20,000+ on secondary markets.
funFacts: The Sky-Dweller took Rolex approximately 10 years to develop from concept to production — an unusually long gestation for a watch company of Rolex's resources.
The Ring Command bezel system is a patented Rolex invention — rotating the bezel selects which complication to set (date, dual time, or neither).
At launch in 2012, the Sky-Dweller was only available in precious metals; Rolex didn't release it in steel until 2017, when it immediately became one of the most-wanted models.
The annual calendar in the Sky-Dweller uses a patented 'Saros' display with satellite-like months shown around the edge of the dial — a completely novel calendar display.

name: Sea-Dweller
description: The professional saturation diver's watch rated to 1,220 metres. Features a helium escape valve, Cerachrom bezel, and the calibre 3235 in a two-tone Rolesor case.

specs.strapMaterial: Oystersteel and 18k yellow gold Oyster bracelet

history: The Sea-Dweller emerged from Rolex's partnership with COMEX (Compagnie Maritime d'Expertises), a French underwater engineering company that conducted commercial deep-sea operations. Professional divers needed watches that could withstand the helium gas used in saturation diving environments — helium atoms are small enough to penetrate standard watch seals and cause the crystal to blow off during decompression. Rolex's patented helium escape valve solved this. The Sea-Dweller was first certified to 610m in 1967.
highestSalePrice: A rare Rolex Sea-Dweller ref. 1665 'Great White' with a rare single red line dial sold at Phillips for $333,000. Vintage single and double Red Sea-Dweller models are among the most coveted Rolex pieces in collector circles.
funFacts: The Sea-Dweller's helium escape valve was a genuine engineering solution to a real problem — saturation divers working in helium-oxygen environments needed a way to vent trapped helium during decompression.
COMEX diving operations in the North Sea used Sea-Dwellers in harsh industrial conditions — some of those watches have surfaced at auction with remarkable provenance.
The 'Single Red' and 'Double Red' Sea-Dweller models (named for the text color on the dial) from the late 1960s are considered among the most important Rolex collectibles.
The current Sea-Dweller ref. 126603 features the Calibre 3235, 70-hour power reserve, and a Cerachrom bezel — all while remaining pressure-tested to 600m.

name: Speedmaster Moonwatch Professional
description: The legendary Moonwatch — first watch worn on the Moon during Apollo 11. Features the manually wound calibre 3861, Master Chronometer certified with Hesalite crystal.
history: The Speedmaster Professional 'Moonwatch' is the only watch certified by NASA for all crewed space missions. In 1965, after rigorous testing against eleven competitor watches — including extreme temperature cycling, pressure, humidity, shock, and vibration tests — only the Speedmaster survived. On July 20, 1969, Buzz Aldrin wore a Speedmaster on the lunar surface while Neil Armstrong left his in the Eagle lunar module as a backup timer after the onboard clock malfunctioned. The Moonwatch has flown on every NASA crewed mission since.
highestSalePrice: The Speedmaster used by Apollo 13 astronaut Jack Swigert to time the critical engine burn that brought the crew home after the oxygen tank explosion sold at auction for $1,773,000 at RR Auction in 2015. A Speedmaster Professional ref. 105.003 from 1969 with provenance from the moon landing sold for $2,381,562 at Antiquorum.
funFacts: On Apollo 13, the onboard computer failed after the oxygen tank explosion; astronaut Jack Swigert used his Speedmaster to time the critical 14-second engine burn that corrected their trajectory and saved their lives.

NASA tested 11 different watches in 1965 including Rolex, Longines, Hamilton, and Bulova — only the Omega Speedmaster passed all eight tests, largely due to its manual winding and hesalite (acrylic) crystal that doesn't shatter under pressure changes.
The Speedmaster's case design has remained essentially unchanged since 1959 — the 'Moonwatch' uses the same aesthetic as watches worn in the 1960s space program.

Buzz Aldrin didn't wear the Speedmaster on the first steps on the moon — he wore it on the lunar surface about 2.5 hours into the EVA after Armstrong had already stepped out; Armstrong's watch stayed in the LEM as backup timer.

name: Seamaster Diver 300M
description: The James Bond watch since GoldenEye. Features Master Chronometer calibre 8800, ceramic wave-pattern dial, and ceramic bezel with liquidmetal diving scale.
history: The Seamaster Diver 300M became the world's most famous spy watch when Pierce Brosnan wore it as James Bond in 'GoldenEye' (1995), ending the Rolex era in the Bond franchise. Omega has been Bond's watch ever since, continuing with Timothy Dalton, Daniel Craig's entire run, and beyond. The current generation features a co-axial escapement developed by George Daniels and adopted by Omega in 1999 — the most significant development in mechanical watch movements in decades.
highestSalePrice: A Seamaster Diver 300M with documented Bond film provenance from 'Casino Royale' sold for $254,000 at Christie's. The standard modern Seamaster retails at approximately $5,900 with strong secondary market performance.
funFacts: The decision to put Bond in an Omega rather than Rolex in 1995 was a watershed moment — it shifted the brand's prestige significantly and is widely cited as the most effective product placement in watch history.
The Seamaster 300M's wave-pattern dial is inspired by the horizontal lines visible when you look down through clear ocean water — a design detail that recurs in multiple Omega dive watches.
In 'Die Another Day' (2002), Bond's Seamaster contained a hidden grappling hook and mines — Omega protested to the filmmakers that this was too far-fetched even for Bond.

The co-axial escapement, developed by master watchmaker George Daniels and licensed to Omega, significantly reduces friction compared to traditional lever escapements, extending service intervals to 8–10 years.

name: Seamaster Planet Ocean 600M
description: Professional dive watch rated to 600m with unidirectional rotating bezel in ceramic. Features the Master Chronometer calibre 8900 with 15,000 gauss magnetic resistance.
history: The Seamaster Planet Ocean 600M was introduced in 2005 as the most extreme professional diving watch in Omega's line-up. The 600m water resistance — achieved through a helium escape valve and robust case construction — places it at the boundary between professional and saturation diving capability. Its most famous mission was as the watch worn by the James Bond character in Casino Royale (2006), where it appeared prominently in the underwater fight scene.
highestSalePrice: A Planet Ocean worn during the filming of 'Casino Royale' with production documentation sold for $28,750 at Bonhams. The standard retail price is approximately $7,200.
funFacts: The Planet Ocean was developed in consultation with the CMAS (World Underwater Federation) to ensure it met professional diving standards beyond typical sport diving requirements.
Its unidirectional rotating bezel can be operated with diving gloves — a design requirement that drove the raised, textured grip pattern.
Omega tested the Planet Ocean at simulated depths of 600m using hyperbaric chambers — equivalent to pressures nearly 60 times greater than at sea level.
The name 'Planet Ocean' refers to Earth itself — when viewed from space, the planet is predominantly blue ocean, covering approximately 71% of its surface.

name: Aqua Terra 150M
description: Versatile luxury sports watch with distinctive teak-pattern dial inspired by the wooden decks of sailing yachts. Master Chronometer calibre 8900 with anti-magnetic properties.
history: The Aqua Terra was introduced in 2002 as Omega's most versatile watch — equally at home at a business meeting or on a sailing boat. Its defining feature is the horizontal 'teak concept' dial with vertical lines evoking the teak wood decks of luxury yachts. The METAS-certified Master Chronometer version passes eight stringent tests including resistance to magnetic fields of 15,000 gauss — rendering standard MRI machines harmless to the movement.
highestSalePrice: Aqua Terra watches are production timepieces that trade near retail; well-preserved early examples with rare dials reach $3,000–$5,000 at auction.
funFacts: The Aqua Terra's Master Chronometer certification means it has passed all eight tests mandated by METAS — the Swiss Federal Institute of Metrology — including a 15,000 gauss magnetic field test.
The 'teak concept' dial pattern was inspired by the horizontal planks of traditional yacht decks — a design language shared across the Aqua Terra collection.
The modern Aqua Terra uses a silicon balance spring — silicon is non-magnetic and more stable than traditional metal springs, contributing to extreme accuracy.
Omega has made the Aqua Terra the official watch of multiple America's Cup teams, with special editions commemorating each race series.

name: Constellation Co-Axial Master Chronometer
description: Omega's prestige dress watch with signature claws on the case and pie-pan dial. Features the Master Chronometer calibre 8800 with Co-Axial escapement.
history: The Constellation was introduced in 1952 and was Omega's first watch to be officially chronometer-certified — hence the 'Constellation' name representing the highest standard in precision. Its distinctive 'claws' or 'griffes' holding the sapphire crystal are a signature design element. The Co-Axial Master Chronometer version features the in-house Calibre 8800 with co-axial escapement and anti-magnetic protection.
highestSalePrice: Vintage Constellation models, particularly the 'Pie Pan' dial variants from the 1950s–60s, have sold for $8,000–$20,000. Modern examples trade near retail.
funFacts: The Constellation was the first Omega to achieve Official Swiss Chronometer Testing Institute (COSC) certification — beginning the brand's tradition of precision timing that led to NASA certification.
The 'griffes' (claws) on the case are both aesthetic and functional — they protect the sapphire crystal and are a defining design element that has appeared in various forms since 1982.
Omega has been the official timekeeper of the Olympic Games since 1932 — a relationship that has given the Constellation its association with sporting precision.
The Constellation collection included some of Omega's most unusual vintage watches, including models with solid gold dial overlays and rare stone dials.

name: Speedmaster '57 Co-Axial
description: Vintage-inspired Speedmaster with broad arrow hands and the Master Chronometer calibre 9906. A refined chronograph blending 1957 heritage with modern technology.
history: The Speedmaster '57 Co-Axial Master Chronometer revives the proportions of the original 1957 Speedmaster — the very first model in the Speedmaster line. Unlike the Moonwatch which evolved, the '57 references the original's aesthetic with a rounder case, older dial layout, and period-correct lyre lugs. The Co-Axial Master Chronometer movement brings 21st-century precision to a 1957 silhouette.
highestSalePrice: The Speedmaster '57 is a modern production watch; pristine examples trade at $5,000–$7,000, close to the retail price of approximately $6,600.
funFacts: The original 1957 Speedmaster ref. CK 2915 was designed in just 10 weeks by Claude Baillod for the Swiss watch fair — Omega didn't yet know it would become the most famous space watch in history.
The '57's lyre lugs — the curved attachments that join the case to the bracelet — were standard in 1957 but were replaced with straight lugs in subsequent Speedmaster generations.
The tachymeter scale on the original 1957 Speedmaster was on the bezel from the start — this was unusual at the time, when most chronographs had the scale on the dial.
The CK 2915 (the 1957 original) used a modified Lemania movement; today's Speedmaster '57 uses the in-house Calibre 9906, a completely different movement.

name: Seamaster 300
description: Heritage-inspired dive watch recalling the CK2913 from 1957. Features the Master Chronometer calibre 8912 with sandwich dial and broad arrow hands in a 41mm case.
history: The Seamaster 300 was revived in 2014 as a tribute to the 1957 original Seamaster 300 — Omega's first professional dive watch, launched alongside the Speedmaster and Railmaster in what collectors call the 'trilogy.' The 2014 revival was modernized with co-axial Master Chronometer specifications while maintaining the vintage aesthetic. The ref. 234.30.41.21.01.001 uses the black dial with the distinctive vintage lume plots.
highestSalePrice: The Seamaster 300 trades near retail at approximately $5,400. Vintage original Seamaster 300 examples with tropical dials can fetch $8,000–$15,000 at auction.
funFacts: The 1957 Omega 'trilogy' — Speedmaster, Seamaster 300, and Railmaster — is considered one of the most important product launches in watch history, establishing three distinct watch categories simultaneously.
The original Seamaster 300 was used by military divers and frogmen in the 1960s — its durable construction made it a preferred professional tool.
The modern revival of the Seamaster 300 uses the same case size (41mm) and similar dial layout as the original, with the key difference being the anti-magnetic Master Chronometer movement.
Omega's wave-pattern dial texture, present on many Seamaster models, is absent on the Seamaster 300 — it deliberately uses a simpler, flat dial in keeping with the vintage original.

name: De Ville Prestige
description: Elegant dress watch from Omega's classic collection. Clean design with power reserve indicator and the Co-Axial calibre 2500 in a slim 39.5mm steel case.
history: The De Ville Prestige is Omega's dress watch collection — a descendant of the early Constellation models that were rebranded in 1967. The collection focuses on elegance over function: slim cases, refined dials, and restrained aesthetics. The co-axial version brings precision watchmaking to a classical dress watch package.
highestSalePrice: De Ville watches trade near retail; they represent good value for a Swiss mechanical dress watch with in-house movement. Vintage examples with unusual dials occasionally reach $3,000–$5,000 at specialist auctions.
funFacts: The De Ville name comes from 'de ville' — French for 'of the city' — positioning it as an urban counterpart to Omega's sporting watches.

The co-axial escapement was invented by British master watchmaker George Daniels in 1974 and licensed exclusively to Omega in 1999 — it reduces friction between components by approximately 79% compared to traditional lever escapements.
The De Ville Trésor — a sister model — holds the record for the thinnest Omega mechanical movement in production.
Omega used the De Ville collection to showcase its first co-axial movement to the public in 1999, making the collection historically significant in modern watchmaking.

name: Speedmaster Silver Snoopy Award 50th Anniversary
description: Celebrating the Silver Snoopy Award given to Omega by NASA. Features an animated Snoopy on the caseback that orbits the far side of the moon. One of the most sought-after modern Omegas.
history: The Silver Snoopy Award is NASA's second-highest honor for safety achievements. In 1970, NASA presented Omega with the Silver Snoopy Award for the Speedmaster's role in the survival of Apollo 13 — specifically for Jack Swigert's use of his watch to time the engine burn. Omega commemorates this with special editions; the 50th anniversary edition (2020) features a silver Snoopy on the dial, a miniature astronaut Snoopy on the seconds hand visible through the sapphire caseback, and a movement architecture with the spacecraft trajectory shown via the hand alignment.
highestSalePrice: The Silver Snoopy Award 50th Anniversary is a limited edition commanding strong secondary market premiums; it retails at approximately $9,850 and trades at $12,000–$18,000 due to its limited production and unique features.
funFacts: Every Silver Snoopy Award 50th Anniversary comes with its own 'flight-qualified' Snoopy astronaut figurine in the box — a nod to the actual Silver Snoopy trophies NASA presents.

The movement of this watch has a hidden mechanical Easter egg — when the small seconds hand points at specific positions, the entire astronaut-Snoopy figure visible through the sapphire back 'floats' through a miniature space scene.
Snoopy became NASA's official safety mascot in 1968 — before the moon landing — and remains so today, with the character used in safety campaign materials across all NASA facilities.
Only a handful of companies and individuals have ever received the Silver Snoopy Award from NASA — Omega's 1970 award is one of the most prestigious in the watch industry's history.

name: Speedmaster Racing Co-Axial
description: Motorsport-inspired Speedmaster with distinctive racing dial featuring minute track and Master Chronometer certification. Calibre 9900 with column-wheel chronograph.
history: The Speedmaster Racing Co-Axial pays tribute to Omega's motorsport connections — the brand has been deeply involved in Formula 1 timing since the 1960s. The MoonSwatch collaboration with Swatch in 2022 brought a new generation to the Speedmaster family, but the Racing remains the athletic performance expression within the regular Moonwatch range, featuring skeleton-like dial architecture and bold color accents.
highestSalePrice: The Speedmaster Racing retails at approximately $10,600 and is a production model that trades near retail.
funFacts: The Speedmaster's tachymeter scale — present since 1957 — is a direct reference to motorsport: it measures the average speed of an object over a fixed distance.
Omega supplied Formula 1 timing equipment from the early 1960s through the late 1970s — a relationship that directly influenced the Speedmaster's design.
Michael Schumacher was an Omega brand ambassador and wore Speedmasters throughout his Formula 1 career, cementing the collection's motorsport identity.
The Speedmaster Racing's 44mm case is notably larger than the classic 42mm Moonwatch, giving it a more dramatic presence on the wrist.

name: Nautilus
description: One of the most iconic luxury sports watches, designed by Gerald Genta in 1976 with porthole-shaped case. Discontinued in 2021, making it one of the most coveted references ever.
history: The Nautilus ref. 5711 is arguably the most sought-after steel sports watch of the 21st century. Designed by Gerald Genta and launched in 1976, its porthole-inspired case and integrated bracelet broke every convention of fine watchmaking — it was the first luxury watch to combine the prestige of precious metal finishing with the ruggedness of a sports case. When Patek Philippe discontinued the steel 5711 in January 2021, announcing it would be replaced by a Tiffany-blue dial edition before retirement, a frenzy ensued.
highestSalePrice: The final production Nautilus 5711/1A-010 in Tiffany Blue, of which only 170 examples were made, sold at Phillips New York in December 2021 for $6,503,000 — one of the highest prices ever paid for a production steel watch. Standard 5711 references trade at $100,000–$150,000 on secondary markets against a former retail of ~$35,000.
funFacts: Gerald Genta reportedly designed the Nautilus on a paper napkin in a single night — the brief was to create a luxury sports watch inspired by a ship's porthole.
When the Nautilus launched in 1976, it was priced higher than the Rolex Submariner — an almost unthinkable proposition at the time for a steel watch.
The Nautilus bracelet has concealed clasps — the folding mechanism is invisible when worn, creating a seamlessly integrated look that was revolutionary in 1976.
Patek Philippe's decision to discontinue the steel 5711 caused its secondary market value to triple within months — a price movement unprecedented for a production Swiss watch.

name: Aquanaut
description: The modern luxury sports watch launched in 1997 with its distinctive rounded octagonal case and tropical composite strap. Often called the Nautilus's younger sibling.
history: The Aquanaut was launched in 1997 as a younger, sportier interpretation of the Nautilus concept — designed to appeal to a new generation while keeping Patek Philippe's prestige. Its rounded octagonal case and embossed 'tropical' rubber strap (a unique composite material) give it a more casual, active personality than the Nautilus. The 5167A in steel with the black/grey dial is the entry-level Patek Philippe sports watch and is almost as difficult to obtain as the Nautilus.
highestSalePrice: The Aquanaut Luce Travel Time ref. 5650G in white gold with complications sold for $185,500 at Christie's. Standard steel 5167A examples trade at $50,000–$80,000 on secondary markets against a retail price of approximately $22,000.
funFacts: The Aquanaut's 'tropical' strap is made from a composite material called 'Caoutchouc' — a technical rubber compound developed for Patek Philippe that is resistant to salt water, UV radiation, and extreme temperatures.
The Aquanaut case shape is described by Patek as 'rounded octagonal' — it has eight sides but with significantly curved corners, creating a more relaxed, oval-like appearance.
Unlike the Nautilus which has a horizontal emphasis, the Aquanaut has a more square proportion — historically it sold less well, but recent demand has made it nearly as coveted as the Nautilus.
The Aquanaut dial pattern is embossed in a cross-hatch design that echoes the texture of the strap — one of the rare cases where strap and dial share the same visual language.

name: Calatrava
description: The purest expression of the round dress watch, defining elegance since 1932. The officer's case features a hinged dust cover and the ultra-thin calibre 324 SC.
history: The Calatrava is Patek Philippe's definitive dress watch — understated, classical, and timeless since its introduction in 1932. Named after the cross of the Order of Calatrava (a medieval Spanish knightly order), the round case and minimal complications embody what many consider the Platonic ideal of a dress watch. The ref. 5227G in white gold maintains the Calatrava tradition while adding a discreet caseback that functions as a mirror when closed.
notableWearers: Various heads of state and royalty
highestSalePrice: Vintage Calatrava pieces with exceptional provenance can reach $200,000+ at auction. A rare ref. 570 Calatrava with a 'Tiffany' signed dial and original box sold for $218,500 at Christie's.
funFacts: The Calatrava's design principle is rooted in the Bauhaus idea that 'form follows function' — every element on the dial exists for a reason, and nothing decorative is added without purpose.
Patek Philippe has made the Calatrava in the same fundamental round-case form since 1932 — making it one of the longest continuously produced watch designs in history.
Many of the most important people in 20th-century history wore a Calatrava, including several Nobel Prize winners and heads of government who preferred understated elegance.

The Calatrava cross symbol appears on Patek Philippe's crown — it represents the brand's commitment to the highest standards of craftsmanship, borrowed from the medieval order's reputation for excellence.

name: Annual Calendar
description: Features Patek Philippe's patented annual calendar that requires only one manual correction per year, on March 1st. Moon phase, day, date, and month in an elegant layout.
history: The Annual Calendar is part of Complications, a distinguished Swiss watchmaking collection... The self-winding rotor harvests energy from wrist motion, typically maintaining power reserve from normal daily activity.
funFacts: The Annual Calendar represents Complications's commitment to precision Swiss watchmaking.

name: Perpetual Calendar Chronograph
description: The pinnacle of Patek Philippe's Grand Complications. Combines a perpetual calendar with chronograph in the first PP perpetual calendar chrono with in-house movement.
history: The Perpetual Calendar Chronograph ref. 5270 is widely considered one of the most important complications in watchmaking — combining a perpetual calendar (which automatically accounts for months of different lengths and leap years) with a flyback chronograph. In platinum, the ref. 5270P-001 represents the pinnacle of Patek Philippe watchmaking. The Calibre CH 29-535 PS Q movement took decades to develop and introduced a new column-wheel chronograph mechanism.
highestSalePrice: The most expensive watch ever sold at auction is the Patek Philippe Grandmaster Chime ref. 6300A-010, which achieved CHF 31.19 million ($31 million) at the Only Watch charity auction in Geneva in November 2019. In the perpetual calendar chronograph category, the ref. 5270P regularly achieves $200,000–$400,000 at major auction houses.
funFacts: A perpetual calendar watch needs correction only once every 4 years, at the end of a non-leap year February — compared to standard calendar watches which need adjustment 7 times per year.
Patek Philippe once created a pocket watch — the Graves Supercomplication — that was the most complicated mechanical timepiece ever made (1933). It had 24 complications and took 8 years to complete.
The Calibre CH 29-535 PS Q took Patek Philippe 10 years to develop — a process that involved creating entirely new manufacturing equipment to achieve the required tolerances.

Patek Philippe's slogan — 'You never actually own a Patek Philippe. You merely look after it for the next generation' — refers to this category of watches that appreciate significantly over generations.

name: World Time
description: Displays all 24 time zones simultaneously with cloisonné enamel dial depicting a map of the world. One of the most beautiful complications in the Patek collection.
history: The World Time ref. 5231 displays all 24 time zones simultaneously through a rotating disc system developed by Louis Cottier in the 1930s — and first implemented by Patek Philippe. The current version uses a redesigned Calibre 240 HU movement with an integrated world timer complication. The cloisonné enamel dial versions of the 5231 are masterpieces of decorative art, hand-painted by artisans requiring hundreds of hours.
notableWearers: Diplomats and executives who travel extensively
Watch collectors who appreciate artistic complications
highestSalePrice: A ref. 5231J World Time with a cloisonné enamel dial depicting a specific city sold for $620,000 at Sotheby's — well above the $40,000+ retail price. These enamel versions represent the intersection of fine watchmaking and miniature painting.
funFacts: The world time mechanism was invented by Louis Cottier in Geneva in 1931 — Patek Philippe was among the first to implement it in wristwatches, and has produced the most significant collection of world time watches.
Setting the World Time watch requires a separate pusher at 10 o'clock — pressing it rotates the entire city ring, allowing you to set your local time zone in a single action.
The hand-painted cloisonné enamel variants of the World Time take a single artisan over 200 hours to complete — making them among the most labor-intensive dials in watchmaking.
The world time display shows 24 time zones but lists cities, not country names — the cities were chosen to represent each time zone's dominant region.

name: Perpetual Calendar
description: A classic perpetual calendar in an elegant Calatrava-style case. Automatically accounts for months of different lengths and leap years until 2100.
history: The Perpetual Calendar ref. 5327 in white gold represents the quintessential Patek Philippe complication watch — a perpetual calendar displays the day, date, month, and moon phase while automatically accounting for months of different lengths. The mechanism will correctly display February 29 in leap years and needs correction only at century turns not divisible by 400. It was developed over decades and remains one of the most mechanically elegant complications ever created.
highestSalePrice: Patek Philippe perpetual calendars regularly achieve $80,000–$250,000 at major auctions. A rare ref. 5059 perpetual calendar with a black dial sold for $185,000 at Christie's.
funFacts: A true perpetual calendar mechanism 'knows' the length of each month through a mechanical memory encoded in a series of cams — no batteries, no electronics, just centuries of horological refinement.
The most difficult problem in perpetual calendar design is February — the mechanism must account for both short and leap year Februaries, which requires a complex second-year cam cycle.
Patek Philippe's perpetual calendar movement, the Calibre 240 Q, consists of over 300 individual components — the calendar module alone accounts for 189 of them.
When setting a perpetual calendar, you can only advance it forward — moving it backward risks breaking the delicate calendar mechanism, which is designed to move in only one direction.

name: Nautilus Chronograph
description: The chronograph variant of the iconic Nautilus, featuring the calibre CH 28-520 C flyback chronograph. Combines sporty timing functionality with the Nautilus design language.
history: The Nautilus Chronograph ref. 5980 combines the Nautilus sports case with an integrated flyback chronograph — a technically demanding combination given the integrated bracelet design leaves little room for additional movement components. The result is one of the most coveted Nautilus variants. The ref. 5980/1A in steel is among the most expensive and rare Patek Philippe sports watches in regular production.
highestSalePrice: The Nautilus Chronograph 5980 in steel commanded $80,000–$120,000 on secondary markets. A special edition in rose gold with a blue dial sold for $220,000 at Phillips.
funFacts: The flyback function in the 5980's chronograph allows you to reset and restart the timer with a single push — crucial for professionals who need to time multiple sequential intervals.
Integrating a flyback chronograph into the slender Nautilus case required Patek Philippe to develop an entirely new movement architecture — the Calibre CH 28-520 C.
The Nautilus Chronograph is water-resistant to 120m — more than most chronographs, which typically sacrifice water resistance to accommodate the additional pushers.
At retail, the 5980/1A was priced at approximately $48,000 — but it consistently traded at $80,000+ on secondary markets due to extremely limited production.

name: Calatrava Pilot Travel Time
description: Aviation-inspired Calatrava with dual time zone and local/home time display. Vintage pilot watch aesthetics with Patek Philippe finishing and complications.
history: The Calatrava Pilot Travel Time ref. 5524 is an unusual departure for Patek Philippe — a pilot's-style watch from a brand associated with ultra-formal dress pieces. Its large 42mm white gold case with luminous arabic numerals and dual time zone function via twin crowns (local and home time) represents Patek's interpretation of an aviation tool watch. It was inspired by historical pilot watches from Patek's archives.
notableWearers: Pilot watch enthusiasts with fine watch budgets
highestSalePrice: The 5524G retails at approximately $50,000 and holds value well on secondary markets, with some examples selling at $55,000–$70,000.
funFacts: The Travel Time function allows you to jump the local time hour hand forward or backward in one-hour increments using the two crowns — without disrupting the running seconds.
Patek Philippe's archive research for the 5524 uncovered pilot watches it made in the 1930s for military aviators — the 5524 references this legacy while bringing modern performance.
The arabic numerals on the 5524 dial are a direct reference to historical aviation watches that required maximum legibility in cockpit conditions.
At 42mm, the 5524G is one of the largest watches in Patek Philippe's current catalogue — the brand typically keeps cases in the 38–40mm range.

name: Annual Calendar Moon Phase
description: Elegant annual calendar with moon phase, power reserve indicator, and subsidiary seconds. A cornerstone of the Complications collection.
history: The Annual Calendar ref. 5146 was introduced in 1996, filling the gap between a simple date watch and the far more expensive perpetual calendar. The annual calendar mechanism — invented by Patek Philippe — requires only one manual correction per year (at the end of February) because it automatically distinguishes between 30 and 31-day months but cannot account for the 28/29 days of February.
notableWearers: Watch collectors who appreciate practical complications
highestSalePrice: Annual Calendar models retail at approximately $35,000–$60,000 and perform well at auction; examples with rare dial colors have sold for $80,000+.
funFacts: Patek Philippe invented the annual calendar complication in 1996 — patenting the mechanism that distinguishes 30 from 31-day months automatically.
The annual calendar displays date, day, month, and moon phase on a single dial — what Patek calls a 'triple calendar' with automatic date correction.
The moon phase display is accurate to 1 day in approximately 122 years — meaning the mechanism would require a single 1-day correction only after over a century of running.
Between the annual calendar and perpetual calendar is the 'secular perpetual calendar' — which accounts for century years — but these are extraordinarily rare and found only in one-of-a-kind pieces.

name: Twenty~4 Automatic
description: Patek Philippe's dedicated women's collection. The first automatic Twenty~4, featuring a round case with elegant proportions and the calibre 324 S C.
history: The Twenty~4 Automatic represents Patek Philippe's ladies collection — launched in 1999, the rectangular case references the Tank-style watches of the mid-20th century. The '4910/1200A-001' introduced an automatic movement (Calibre 324 S C) to the collection for the first time in 2021, elevating it from a quartz piece to a genuine manufacture timepiece with an exhibition caseback.
notableWearers: Fashion-forward women who appreciate fine watchmaking
highestSalePrice: The Twenty~4 Automatic retails at approximately $20,000–$30,000 and holds value well. Diamond-set versions in gold can exceed $50,000 at auction.
funFacts: The Twenty~4 name refers to wearing the watch 24 hours a day — positioned as a versatile piece for the modern woman's active lifestyle.

Until 2021, the entire Twenty~4 collection was quartz-powered — unusual for a brand that prides itself on mechanical movements. The addition of the automatic caliber was a major event for female watch enthusiasts.
Patek Philippe's female customer base has grown significantly in the 21st century, with the Twenty~4 playing a central role in attracting women to the brand.
The rectangular case of the Twenty~4 was directly inspired by vintage Patek cartier-style cases from the 1940s and 50s — a deliberate reference to a golden age of watch design.

name: Royal Oak Selfwinding
description: The watch that created the luxury steel sports watch category. Designed by Gerald Genta in 1972, the octagonal bezel with exposed screws and Tapisserie dial are instant icons.
history: The Royal Oak Selfwinding ref. 15500 is the updated successor to the iconic 15202 (itself the successor to the original 5402). At 41mm, it's larger than the 'Jumbo' 39mm but smaller than many modern sports watches. The calibre 4302 inside features a new architecture with 70 hours of power reserve — a significant update from the previous generation's 60 hours. The 15500 was controversial among purists for its slightly updated dial.
highestSalePrice: The 15500ST in blue dial trades at $60,000–$80,000 on secondary markets; specific dial colors like the 'Smurf' blue or 'Soldier' black command higher premiums.
funFacts: The Royal Oak's tapisserie dial pattern is different for each case size — the 41mm uses a different grid scale than the 39mm, maintaining visual balance across sizes.
Audemars Piguet manufactures almost all components of the Royal Oak in-house, including the finishing — the hours of hand-polishing and beveling on a single Royal Oak can exceed 40 hours.

The Royal Oak bracelet is integrated so seamlessly that the clasp is designed to be nearly invisible when viewed from certain angles — the engineering required to create this disappearing clasp took years to perfect.
AP produces fewer than 50,000 watches per year total — making the Royal Oak genuinely scarce despite being the brand's most popular and commercially important model.

name: Royal Oak Chronograph
description: Chronograph variant of the Royal Oak featuring calibre 2385 with column wheel and vertical clutch. Blue Grande Tapisserie dial with silver-toned counters.
history: The Royal Oak Chronograph adds a flyback chronograph complication to the Royal Oak's iconic case — a considerable technical challenge given the integrated bracelet design leaves little internal volume for additional movement components. The result uses the Calibre 2385, one of AP's most sophisticated in-house movements.
highestSalePrice: Royal Oak Chronograph steel models trade at $40,000–$60,000 on secondary markets. Limited editions and precious metal versions can exceed $100,000.
funFacts: The flyback function in the Royal Oak Chronograph allows the timer to be reset and restarted with a single push — ideal for timing multiple sequential laps or intervals.
Fitting a chronograph mechanism into the Royal Oak's slender integrated case required AP to develop a movement with a distinctly thin profile while maintaining the full 40 hours of power reserve.
The Royal Oak Chronograph uses a column-wheel architecture for the chronograph mechanism — considered more precise and pleasant to operate than cam-actuated designs.
AP's calibre 2385 is constructed from 385 individual components — each requiring its own beveling and finishing before assembly.

name: Royal Oak Offshore Chronograph
description: The bold, oversized sibling of the Royal Oak, introduced in 1993. Known as 'The Beast' for its imposing 42mm case with integrated rubber crown guard.
history: The Royal Oak Offshore Chronograph was introduced in 1993 as a larger, more aggressive interpretation of the Royal Oak. Nicknamed 'The Beast' for its then-radical 42mm case, it expanded the Royal Oak family into sports chronograph territory. Designed by Emmanuel Gueit, it amplified the Royal Oak's industrial aesthetic with a wider case, more prominent crown guards, and bolder overall presence. Today it remains one of the most recognizable luxury sports chronographs.
highestSalePrice: Limited edition Royal Oak Offshore models by collaborating artists have sold for $200,000+ at auction. A rare early 'Beast' ref. 25721ST from 1993 sold for $45,000 at Sotheby's.
funFacts: When the Royal Oak Offshore debuted in 1993 at 42mm, it was considered outrageously large for a luxury watch — the industry norm was 36–38mm. Today, 42mm is considered modest.
The 'Offshore' name was chosen to distinguish this larger sports variant from the original — referencing offshore racing, offshore drilling, and extreme environments.
AP collaborated with numerous artists and brands for limited edition Offshore versions — including Millenary fine art editions, Team Alinghi sailing editions, and Royal Salute whisky editions.
The Offshore's 'Méga Tapisserie' pattern uses a much larger grid than the standard Royal Oak's tapisserie — creating a coarser, more ruggedized appearance that matches the watch's muscular character.

name: Royal Oak Perpetual Calendar
description: One of the thinnest perpetual calendar watches available. Displays day, date, month, moon phase, and week number in the iconic Royal Oak case.
history: The Royal Oak Perpetual Calendar combines the iconic sports watch with one of horology's most revered complications. The perpetual calendar mechanism in a waterproof, sporting context required significant engineering effort — the pushers for adjusting the calendar had to be waterproofed without adding bulk to the case. The result is a watch that can accompany its owner to any environment while displaying the most practical complex calendar in existence.
notableWearers: High-achieving professionals who want a single exceptional watch
Royal Oak collectors seeking the ultimate in complication
highestSalePrice: The Royal Oak Perpetual Calendar in steel retails at approximately $55,000 and trades at $80,000–$120,000 on secondary markets.
funFacts: Combining a perpetual calendar with a water-resistant sports watch required AP to redesign the corrector pushers to be both waterproof and operable — a significant technical challenge.
The Royal Oak Perpetual Calendar's mechanism needs correction only at century turns not divisible by 400 — the next correction won't be needed until the year 2100.
AP uses a moonphase display that achieves accuracy of 1 day every 122 years — matching Patek Philippe's standard, which had long been considered the best in the industry.
The perpetual calendar Royal Oak maintains the same water resistance as the standard model (50m) — a feat that required entirely rethinking the crown and pusher sealing system.

name: Royal Oak Jumbo Extra-Thin
description: The purest Royal Oak, faithful to Genta's 1972 original. Ultra-thin 8.1mm case with calibre 7121 and Petite Tapisserie dial.
history: The Royal Oak Jumbo Extra-Thin ref. 16202 is the closest modern watch to the legendary 1972 original. Gerald Genta's design — created overnight on a paper napkin after a challenge from AP's then-CEO Georges Golay — was radical: a steel luxury sports watch with integrated bracelet and exposed screws on the case. The 'Jumbo' nickname references the original's relative size. The 16202 was introduced at AP's 150th anniversary in 2022 and features an ultra-thin in-house movement.
highestSalePrice: An original 1972 Audemars Piguet Royal Oak ref. 5402 in steel sold at Sotheby's for $426,000. The modern 16202 trades at $120,000–$200,000 on secondary markets against a retail price of approximately $26,000 — one of the largest retail-to-secondary premiums in watchmaking.
funFacts: Gerald Genta drew the Royal Oak design in a single night — the brief from AP's CEO was to create a luxury steel sports watch in time for a 1972 trade show, with about 24 hours notice.

The eight hexagonal screws on the Royal Oak bezel are decorative — they once served a functional purpose in early dive watch designs, but on the Royal Oak they reference industrial machinery and diving equipment aesthetics.
When the Royal Oak launched in 1972, it was priced at 3,300 Swiss francs — more expensive than a solid gold Patek Philippe Calatrava. Dealers initially couldn't sell them.
The integrated bracelet's 'tapisserie' pattern on the dial — the tiny squares in the cross-hatch design — requires over 30 separate machining operations and individual polishing steps per dial.

name: Royal Oak Offshore Diver
description: Professional dive watch rated to 300m with inner rotating bezel. Features the calibre 3120 automatic movement in a 42mm steel case.
history: The Royal Oak Offshore Diver brings the Offshore's bold aesthetic into a pure diving watch format — water-resistant to 300m with a unidirectional rotating bezel and diving-optimized display. It preserves the Offshore's distinctive 'mega tapisserie' dial pattern while adding the functional elements needed for underwater use.
highestSalePrice: The Offshore Diver retails at approximately $26,000 and trades at $28,000–$35,000 on secondary markets.
funFacts: At 42mm with 300m water resistance, the Royal Oak Offshore Diver combines dive watch utility with haute horlogerie finishing — a combination almost no other maker achieves at this level.
The rotating bezel on the Offshore Diver uses a ceramic insert — scratch-resistant and color-stable — in keeping with modern professional diving watch standards.
AP offers the Offshore Diver in fluorescent color combinations for maximum underwater visibility — a practical feature that also makes for distinctive fashion statements.
Every Royal Oak Offshore Diver is pressure-tested in water at AP's manufacture before shipping — not just computer-simulated testing, but actual water immersion.

name: Royal Oak Double Balance Wheel Openworked
description: Showcases AP's double balance wheel visible through the openworked dial. Two balance wheels coupled together for improved precision and stability.
history: The Royal Oak Double Balance Wheel Openworked reveals the movement through a fully skeletonized dial, with the centerpiece being the double balance wheel visible at 12 o'clock. The double balance wheel — two linked oscillating masses — improves accuracy by averaging out positional errors. The result is both a horological innovation and an aesthetic spectacle.
highestSalePrice: The Double Balance Wheel Openworked retails at approximately $45,000 and trades at $55,000–$75,000 on secondary markets.
funFacts: The double balance wheel system uses two connected balance wheels that compensate for each other's positional errors — improving accuracy by approximately 4x compared to a single balance.

The skeletonized movement visible through the dial is finished by hand to the same standard as a closed-back movement — a process that takes longer because every surface must be finished from multiple angles.
AP's craftspeople use traditional hand tools — including files and scrapers developed in the 18th century — to achieve the chamfered anglage (beveling) on every component edge.

The double balance wheel innovation was first developed by Louis Brandt & Frère in the 1800s and refined by several modern makers; AP's implementation in the Royal Oak case brought it to mainstream luxury watchmaking.

name: CODE 11.59 Selfwinding Chronograph
description: AP's new collection launched in 2019 with an innovative double-curved sapphire crystal and octagonal middle case. Houses the integrated chronograph calibre 4401.
history: The Code 11.59 Chronograph launched in 2019 as AP's attempt to create a new collection from scratch for the first time in decades. Its circular case with octagonal middle section and arcuate (curved) lugs deliberately departs from the Royal Oak's angular aesthetic. The rose gold reference with leather strap is the most traditional-looking member of the collection.
notableWearers: AP loyalists who wanted a more classical look
highestSalePrice: Code 11.59 references trade near retail, generally at $30,000–$70,000 depending on precious metal content and complications.
funFacts: The Code 11.59's name references the minute before midnight — 'the most important minute of the day' — symbolizing AP's attempt to reinvent itself before a new era.
The watch was initially criticized by many collectors for its styling, which they found derivative — but it has grown in acceptance as a genuine attempt at creating a new classic.
The Code 11.59's movement is entirely new — the Calibre 4401 is AP's first in-house flyback chronograph movement, developed specifically for this collection.
The case of the Code 11.59 uses a 'double curve' sapphire crystal — curved in two planes — which requires specialized equipment to manufacture and polish.

name: CODE 11.59 Selfwinding
description: The time-only CODE 11.59 with double-curved crystal creating a mesmerizing depth effect on the lacquered dial. Calibre 4302 automatic movement.
history: The Code 11.59 Selfwinding ref. 15210 in rose gold with leather strap represents the time-only version of the Code 11.59 — simpler in terms of complications but equally bold in its case design philosophy. The Calibre 4302 inside was newly developed for this collection.
notableWearers: AP collectors seeking something beyond the Royal Oak
highestSalePrice: Code 11.59 in rose gold retails at approximately $25,000–$30,000 and holds value near retail.
funFacts: The Code 11.59 case is more complex to manufacture than the Royal Oak — the alternating convex and straight case sides require different finishing techniques within millimeters of each other.
AP offers the Code 11.59 Selfwinding with a choice of integrated bracelet or strap — a flexibility not offered on the Royal Oak, which comes primarily in integrated bracelet form.
The 'arcuate' lugs of the Code 11.59 are curved in two planes — up toward the dial and outward toward the wrist — a shape that required entirely new case-making machinery.
Initial reception of the Code 11.59 at its 2019 launch was mixed, but subsequent limited editions with unusual materials and dials have been significantly better received.

name: Santos de Cartier Medium
description: One of the most historically significant wristwatches, created in 1904 for aviator Alberto Santos-Dumont. Features QuickSwitch interchangeable bracelet/strap system.
history: The Santos de Cartier is the world's first purpose-designed wristwatch for a man. In 1904, Louis Cartier created it for his friend Alberto Santos-Dumont, the Brazilian aviation pioneer who complained that checking his pocket watch during flights was impractical. The Santos became iconic — its square case, exposed screws, and leather strap set the template for men's wristwatches. The modern WSSA0018 offers both a steel bracelet and leather strap with a patented Quick Switch system allowing the wearer to swap between them in seconds.
highestSalePrice: A unique Santos pocket watch made for Alberto Santos-Dumont personally sold at Christie's for $350,000. The modern Santos in steel with steel bracelet retails at approximately $7,350 and trades near retail.
funFacts: Alberto Santos-Dumont was flying his airship over Paris in 1901 when he first complained to Louis Cartier about the impracticality of his pocket watch — the Santos de Cartier was the result.

Santos-Dumont was one of the most celebrated figures of the early aviation age — in Brazil he is credited with the first powered airplane flight (preceding the Wright Brothers by their account), and the Santos watch remains a national symbol.
The Santos's exposed screws — originally functional, holding the bezel in place — became one of watchmaking's most imitated design details.
The QuickSwitch system on the modern Santos took Cartier several years to engineer — allowing bracelet/strap changes in under a minute without tools is technically more complex than it appears.

name: Tank Must
description: One of the most iconic watch designs in history, inspired by the aerial view of a Renault tank during WWI. Created by Louis Cartier in 1917.
history: The Tank was designed by Louis Cartier in 1917, inspired by the Renault FT tanks he saw deployed on the Western Front of World War I — the parallel lines of the case represent the tracks of the tank's caterpillar drive. It was first gifted to General John Pershing, commander of the American Expeditionary Forces in Europe. The Tank Must (the current model) brings the iconic design to stainless steel with a lacquered dial, making it Cartier's most accessible Tank.
highestSalePrice: Jackie Kennedy's Cartier Tank Américaine, gifted by brother-in-law Prince Stanislas Radziwill, sold at Christie's for $379,500. Andy Warhol reportedly bought multiple Tanks and wore one every day until his death; the specific watches from his collection have sold for $200,000–$700,000.
funFacts: Andy Warhol famously wore only inexpensive Cartier Tank watches — he reportedly said, 'I don't wear a watch to tell the time, I wear it as jewelry.' He consistently wore the Tank Louis.

The Tank has been worn by more American presidents, royalty, and cultural icons than perhaps any other watch design in history — a list including Princess Diana, Queen Elizabeth, Gary Grant, and countless others.

When Princess Diana wore her Tank watch to official engagements, it was noted as unusually sporty for royal protocol — at the time, women's watches were expected to be jeweled and precious metal only.
The Tank's design has barely changed in over 100 years — the proportions, dial layout, and bracelet attachment remain recognizable from 1917 through to today.

name: Tank Francaise
description: A bracelet-integrated reinterpretation of the Tank, introduced in 1996 with curved case that flows seamlessly into the metal bracelet.
history: The Tank Française was introduced in 1996 as a more contemporary, sporting interpretation of the Tank family. Its distinctive feature is the integrated steel bracelet — each link echoing the rectangular case shape — creating a flowing, cohesive wrist presence. Unlike the original Tank's leather strap, the Tank Française was conceived as an integrated bracelet watch from the beginning.
highestSalePrice: The Tank Française retails at approximately $4,300–$6,500 for steel versions; gold variants trade significantly higher.
funFacts: The Tank Française's bracelet links are each individually shaped to mirror the rectangular case profile — creating a sense of continuity from case to bracelet that few integrated designs achieve.
The 'Française' suffix distinguishes it from other Tank variants — it references the French origin of the Cartier maison and positions it as the quintessentially Parisian sports watch.

Cartier introduced the Tank Française in the mid-1990s during a resurgence of interest in watches as fashion accessories — it was perfectly timed for that era's obsession with integrated bracelet sports watches.

The modern Tank Française reintroduced in 2023 features a thinner profile and updated bracelet construction compared to the original — a conscious modernization while maintaining the essential character.

name: Ballon Bleu 36mm
description: Distinctive round case with a blue sapphire cabochon crown that appears to float within the case. One of Cartier's most popular modern designs.
history: The Ballon Bleu was introduced in 2007 and became an instant commercial success — its spherical crown guard (the 'blue balloon' after which it's named) is unique in watchmaking and immediately recognizable. The round case with the distinctive winding crown integration broke from Cartier's rectangular tradition. The 36mm reference targets a unisex audience, sitting between the traditionally men's and women's sizing.
highestSalePrice: The Ballon Bleu is a high-volume production watch that generally trades near retail. Special editions with complications (tourbillon, perpetual calendar) in precious metals have sold for $50,000–$150,000 at auction.
funFacts: The 'blue balloon' in the name refers to the convex sapphire crystal that encloses the crown — an innovation that protects the winding crown while becoming the watch's defining feature.
The Ballon Bleu was designed by Carole Forestier-Kasapi, the head of Cartier's movement creation department — one of the few luxury watch designs attributed to a female watchmaker.
Cartier received multiple design awards for the Ballon Bleu's unique crown protection system — the convex sapphire dome over the crown was entirely novel and required new manufacturing techniques.

Within five years of its 2007 launch, the Ballon Bleu had become one of Cartier's top-selling models globally — a remarkable achievement for a totally new design in a category dominated by established classics.

name: Panthère de Cartier
description: Relaunched in 2017, the Panthère is a jewelry watch icon from the 1980s. Art Deco-inspired square case with soft chain-link bracelet.
history: The Panthère de Cartier has a complex history — originally introduced in 1983 as a women's watch, it became iconic in the 1980s and '90s before being discontinued, then triumphantly revived in 2017. Its square case with rounded corners and integrated mesh-like bracelet is built around the panther (panthère), Cartier's heraldic animal since Jeanne Toussaint — Cartier's creative director — made it the brand's signature in the 1950s.
highestSalePrice: Princess Diana's personal Cartier Panthère, gifted by her stepmother Raine Spencer and worn regularly in the 1980s, sold at Christie's for $191,700.
funFacts: Princess Diana wore her Panthère to events ranging from state dinners to polo matches — helping cement it as the most prestigious women's watch of the late 1980s.
When Cartier discontinued the Panthère in the early 2000s, vintage examples continued to command strong prices — proving the design's enduring appeal and eventually prompting the 2017 revival.
The Cartier panther (panthère) motif was created by Jeanne Toussaint, Cartier's creative director from 1933 to 1970, who was nicknamed 'La Panthère' for her fierce character.

The Panthère's bracelet links are individually assembled and cannot be resized at home — Cartier's service centers maintain specific tools to add or remove the tiny links that give the bracelet its distinctive drape.

name: Santos-Dumont
description: A thin, elegant version of the Santos paying tribute to the aviator. Hand-wound calibre 430 MC in a refined large model case.
history: The Santos-Dumont is the dress watch version of the Santos family — thinner, more elegant, and without the integrated bracelet. It maintains the Santos's square case and exposed screws but in a dress watch format. The XL model suits large wrists while maintaining the Santos-Dumont's essential lightness and elegance.
notableWearers: Watch enthusiasts who prefer square dress watches
highestSalePrice: Santos-Dumont models in gold retail at $15,000–$25,000 and trade near retail.
funFacts: The Santos-Dumont was produced in extremely limited numbers in the early 20th century — the few examples that survive from that era are among the most historically significant wristwatches in existence.
Unlike the sporty Santos with steel bracelet, the Santos-Dumont was always conceived as a dress watch — worn with formal attire to the social functions that Santos-Dumont frequented in Paris.
Alberto Santos-Dumont's personal Santos watches are preserved in Brazilian museums — they are national treasures commemorating one of Brazil's greatest historical figures.
The Santos-Dumont houses a hand-wound movement (Calibre 430 MC) rather than an automatic — reflecting the slimmer design priority of a dress watch over the self-winding convenience.

name: Drive de Cartier
description: Cushion-shaped case with refined proportions for the modern gentleman. Features the calibre 1904-PS MC automatic movement with small seconds.
history: The Drive de Cartier was introduced in 2016 as a distinctly masculine Cartier dress watch — a cushion-shaped case with tonneau-like curves and an integrated alligator strap. Named for the pleasure of driving, it was designed to appeal to men who found the Tank too feminine and the Santos too sporty. The flying tourbillon version houses a remarkable in-house movement.
notableWearers: Collectors of Cartier men's dress watches
highestSalePrice: Drive de Cartier models in steel retail at approximately $6,000; precious metal versions with complications reach $30,000–$80,000.
funFacts: The Drive's cushion case shape is described by Cartier as 'a cushion within a cushion' — with the soft outer profile contrasting with a more structured inner geometry.
The flying tourbillon version of the Drive uses Cartier's Calibre 9452 MC — with the tourbillon cage visible through a display at 6 o'clock and made entirely in-house.
The Drive was the first entirely new Cartier men's watch silhouette in several years — it joined the Tank, Santos, and Ballon Bleu as the fourth pillar of the men's collection.
The name 'Drive' references both the lifestyle of driving luxury automobiles and the act of manual winding — a purposeful ambiguity typical of Cartier's marketing.

name: Pasha de Cartier
description: Distinctive round watch with screw-down crown cap attached by chain, inspired by the Pasha of Marrakech. Relaunched with interchangeable straps and SmartLink bracelet.
history: The Pasha de Cartier was born in 1943 when the Pasha of Marrakech requested a waterproof watch he could wear while swimming in his pool. Cartier created a round watch with a distinctive screwed crown cap attached by a chain — preventing loss of the crown while swimming. After a discontinuation, it was revived in 2020 with updated interchangeable straps and a fresh modern aesthetic.
notableWearers: El Glaoui, Pasha of Marrakech (original owner)
highestSalePrice: A vintage Pasha with original provenance from the Moroccan court could be extraordinarily valuable; documented examples have sold for $50,000–$150,000. The modern revival retails at approximately $5,100 for steel.
funFacts: The Pasha was originally a commission — the actual Pasha of Marrakech walked into Cartier's Paris boutique and asked for a watch he could wear while swimming.
The chain-attached crown cap is functional — it prevents the screw-down crown from getting lost when removed for winding.
Elton John is reportedly one of the most prolific Pasha collectors, amassing dozens of examples from different eras — his collection includes examples with diamonds and colored stones.
The Pasha's round case was unusual for Cartier at the time of its 1943 creation — Cartier's reputation rested on the Tank and other rectangular designs, making the Pasha an unusual commission.

name: Ronde Must de Cartier
description: Cartier's classic round watch in an accessible format. Clean design with Roman numeral dial and blue sword-shaped hands.
history: The Ronde Must de Cartier represents Cartier's most accessible collection — 'Must' refers to Cartier's strategy from the 1970s of making aspirational Cartier pieces at more reachable price points. The steel case with a simple silver dial and classic roman numerals distills Cartier's design language to its essence.
highestSalePrice: The Ronde Must retails at approximately $2,500–$3,500 and trades near retail — it is the entry point to the Cartier world.
funFacts: The 'Must de Cartier' line was created in 1973 by Cartier CEO Robert Hocq as a way to capture the aspirational market — making Cartier accessible at a time when the brand was perceived as exclusively for the very rich.

The Roman numeral hour markers on the Ronde Must are a direct reference to the clock face tradition in French fine jewelry making — Cartier placed roman numerals on their timepieces well before it became a widespread convention.
The blue sword-shaped hands on the Ronde Must are a Cartier signature — appearing across the Tank, Santos, Ballon Bleu, and Ronde collections as a unifying design element.
Cartier's distinctive 'secret signature' — a small 'Cartier' text hidden within the Arabic numeral '7' on the dial — is present on all genuine examples and is a key authentication point.

name: Prospex 62MAS Reinterpretation
description: Modern reinterpretation of Seiko's first professional dive watch from 1965. Slim 13.2mm profile with 6R35 movement and 70-hour power reserve.
history: The Prospex 62MAS Reinterpretation celebrates the 55th anniversary of Japan's first domestic diving watch — the 1965 Seiko 62MAS, designed for Japan Self-Defense Forces diving units. The '62' refers to the caliber number; 'MAS' refers to the Maritime Self-Defense Force. Japan's first divers' watch was produced to compete with Swiss offerings at a time when Japanese manufacturing was proving itself to the world.
highestSalePrice: An original 1965 Seiko 62MAS in exceptional condition sold for $18,000 at Yahoo Auctions Japan. The modern SPB143 retails at approximately $550–$700.
funFacts: Japan's Maritime Self-Defense Force requested the 62MAS in 1965 to reduce dependence on foreign (Swiss) diving watches — it was a matter of national industrial pride as much as practical need.

The 62MAS case was made entirely in Japan — a remarkable achievement in 1965 when Switzerland dominated precision watchmaking. The quality demonstrated by this watch changed international perceptions of Japanese manufacturing.
The 62MAS's case shape — with its unique crown guard and stepped lugs — was designed specifically to withstand the rigors of military dive operations, not consumer use.

Seiko's dive watch heritage culminated in the 1968 Professional 300m Diver — worn by Satoru Makino on JAMSTEC's research dives — establishing Seiko as a serious supplier of underwater scientific instruments.

name: Presage SARB033
description: Widely regarded as one of the greatest value propositions in watchmaking. Reliable 6R15 movement, beautifully finished black dial. Discontinued but legendary.
history: The SARB033 is perhaps the most beloved affordable automatic watch ever made. A 38mm dress watch with the Calibre 6R15 movement, it represented an extraordinary value proposition: Swiss-equivalent quality at a fraction of the price. When Seiko discontinued it in 2019, the watch community erupted — prices on the secondary market tripled immediately. It remains a benchmark for value-focused watch buying.
notableWearers: Reddit's r/Watches community considers it a foundational recommendation
